A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

William James

1842 – 1910

The stream of thought, the self, and a psychology that begins from the inside.

William James does not give us a single treatise called “philosophy of mind.” He gives us something more interesting: a psychology that repeatedly presses beyond psychology into metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, religion, and the theory of agency. A comprehensive Jamesian philosophy of mind therefore has to be extracted from at least four overlapping corpora: The Principles of Psychology and Psychology: Briefer Course; the essays later grouped under radical empiricism, especially “Does Consciousness Exist?” and “A World of Pure Experience”; the religious and psychical writings, including The Varieties of Religious Experience and Human Immortality; and the pragmatic writings, where the mind is treated less as a spectator than as an organ of selection, action, risk, and belief.

The short formulation is this: James’s mature view is a pluralistic, anti-reductionist, embodied, process-oriented, functional, and radically empirical philosophy of mind. Mental life is not a sealed inner theater populated by atomic ideas, nor a soul-substance transparently accessible behind experience, nor a secretion of the brain. It is a temporally flowing, selective, purposive, affective, bodily entangled, socially articulated field of experience. In empirical psychology James treats mental life as correlated with bodily and neural conditions; in metaphysics he later denies that “consciousness” names a separate entity at all, proposing instead “pure experience” as the neutral stuff out of which “mental” and “physical” orders are functionally differentiated. His position about the soul is correspondingly subtle: the soul is not a scientific explanatory posit needed by psychology, but neither is James’s philosophy a dogmatic materialism. In Human Immortality and Varieties he leaves room for transmissive, subliminal, religious, and even post-mortem hypotheses, provided they are treated as metaphysical or experiential possibilities rather than as cheap substitutes for psychological explanation.

James’s importance here is not incidental. He was trained in physiology and medicine, taught one of the earliest American psychology courses and laboratory courses, wrote the monumental Principles of Psychology in 1890, published the shorter Psychology: Briefer Course in 1892, delivered Human Immortality in 1898, delivered the lectures that became The Varieties of Religious Experience in 1901–1902, and in 1904–1905 published the radical-empiricist essays in which he explicitly denied consciousness as a substantial entity.

What follows is a systematic reconstruction.

⸻

1. James’s starting point: psychology as the science of mental life

James opens The Principles of Psychology by defining psychology as “the Science of Mental Life,” where mental life includes “feelings, desires, cognitions, reasonings, decisions, and the like,” studied together with their conditions. That definition is deceptively simple. It does not say psychology is the science of the soul; it does not say psychology is the science of behavior alone; it does not say psychology is the science of brain states. It says psychology studies mental life phenomenally and conditionally: what mental life is like, how it is structured, and under what bodily, environmental, and practical conditions it occurs.

This starting point gives James a disciplined methodological posture. Psychology, for him, is a natural science of individual minds in real time and space. Its first method is introspection, but introspection is not allowed to become armchair metaphysics. It must be supplemented by experiment, comparative inquiry, physiology, pathology, and careful attention to action. James is well aware that introspection is fallible: language distorts mental life, psychologists confuse the object of a thought with the thought itself, and theoretical vocabulary tempts us into treating dynamic transitions as static things. His famous warning against “the psychologist’s fallacy” is precisely the warning not to import the observer’s analytic categories into the living stream being observed.

This gives us the first principle of James’s philosophy of mind: mental life must be described before it is reduced. James is not anti-scientific; he is anti-premature-reductionist. He wants psychology to stay close to the phenomena: the stream, the felt transitions, the fringe, effort, attention, habit, bodily feeling, emotion, choice, dissociation, religious experience, and the self’s practical continuity. He is suspicious of any theory that achieves neatness by discarding the phenomena that made mind philosophically puzzling in the first place.

⸻

2. Mind is not an inner substance but a function in a world

James’s psychology is fundamentally organismic. Mental life is not an isolated inner glow. It mediates between an organism and its environment. In the early chapters of Principles, he praises Herbert Spencer’s idea that mind involves adjustment to environment, while also insisting that mind is not a passive register of impressions. Mind intervenes between stimulation and reaction. Intelligence appears where a fixed end can be reached by varied means. The practical criterion of mind is not mere sensitivity, but the choice of means toward ends.

This is a proto-functionalist view, though James’s functionalism is richer than much later analytic functionalism. It is not merely the claim that mental states are individuated by causal roles. For James, mental life is adaptive, selective, purposive, bodily, affective, and value-laden. The mind’s work is to carve, emphasize, inhibit, anticipate, and act. He says the mind does not copy a ready-made world; it selects from a chaos of possible impressions and constructs the lived world by attention, interest, and action.

That functional orientation also prevents James from endorsing a purely contemplative conception of consciousness. Mind is for life. Abstractions, concepts, memories, sensations, and emotions matter because they guide conduct. In his radical empiricism, James later says that intellectualization serves life and reaction, and abstractions must “redescend” into the immediate stream of experience.

So the second principle is this: mind is an active, selective, world-involving process, not a spectator substance.

⸻

3. Brain, body, and the refusal of crude materialism

James is a physiological psychologist. He accepts that in ordinary embodied life there is no mental event without some neural condition. He calls this the principle that there is “no psychosis without neurosis,” meaning no mental occurrence without a corresponding nervous occurrence. Yet he refuses to conclude from this that thought is simply produced by the brain in the way bile is produced by the liver. He explicitly criticizes the “brain secretes thought” analogy as crude and explanatorily empty.

This distinction is central. James is not denying brain-dependence. He is denying that brain-dependence, by itself, entails reductive materialism. The brain is condition, organ, relay, filter, or correlate; whether it is metaphysically the producer of consciousness is a further hypothesis. This distinction becomes explicit in Human Immortality, where James argues that the observed dependence of mental life on the brain is compatible with different metaphysical interpretations: production, transmission, release, permission, or filtering.

In Principles, James takes brain science seriously. He discusses cortical localization, sensory and motor centers, aphasia, visual function, association tracts, and habit. But his view of the brain is dynamic and distributed rather than narrowly modular. He rejects overly simple localizationism, describing cortical function as mixed, interdependent, and bound up with streams of innervation. The cortex is the practical organ of consciousness in human life, but its operations are not intelligible as isolated points mechanically producing isolated ideas.

Habit is especially important. James treats habit as the bodily sedimentation of past action: the nervous system acquires pathways, and repeated conduct becomes easier, more automatic, and more efficient. Habit therefore shows how mind is embodied historically. We are not merely conscious subjects looking out at the world; we are organisms whose past acts have reorganized our future possibilities.

The third principle is therefore: mind is embodied and neurally conditioned, but not thereby reduced to neural production. James’s philosophy is neither Cartesian dualism nor eliminative materialism. It is a methodological physiological psychology combined with a metaphysically open anti-reductionism.

A recent German scholarly treatment emphasizes this point in contemporary terms: James links naturalistic psychology to metaphysical questions about mind-body relations, mechanism, teleology, and freedom; the nervous system is not a mere mechanism producing discrete mental states, but part of a dynamic organism-environment system.

⸻

4. Against epiphenomenalism: consciousness makes a difference

James’s critique of the “automaton theory” is one of the most important parts of his philosophy of mind. The automaton theory says consciousness is causally idle: bodily processes do all the real work, while consciousness merely accompanies them like foam on a wave or a melody floating above machinery. James recognizes the attraction of this view, especially for physiologists, but he rejects it as biologically and phenomenologically implausible.

His argument is broadly evolutionary and functional. Consciousness appears most developed where behavior becomes complex, uncertain, and adaptive. It seems to arise where discrimination, selection, hesitation, and choice are needed. If consciousness were wholly useless, its systematic connection with life’s most complex adjustments would be strange. James does not present this as a deductive proof, but as a powerful inference: consciousness looks like an organ of selection.

This is also linked to James’s account of will. At the end of his chapter on will, he allows that mechanical accounts may explain much, but he still treats the psychic side as codeterminant. The soul, or more cautiously the conscious side of life, “selects” and is not merely an inert accompaniment.

The fourth principle is: consciousness is efficacious through selection, attention, inhibition, and consent. James’s mind is not a ghostly byproduct; it is a practical factor in the organism’s life.

⸻

5. The stream of thought: James’s phenomenological core

The “stream of consciousness” is James’s best-known contribution, but it is often flattened into a literary phrase. In Principles, it is a rigorous anti-atomistic thesis. James says the starting fact is not isolated sensations or ideas but that “thinking of some sort goes on.” From this he derives five features of thought: thought is personal; thought is constantly changing; thought is sensibly continuous; thought deals with objects independent of itself; and thought is selective.

Each feature matters.

First, thought is personal. Experiences occur within streams. They do not float in a neutral warehouse waiting to be assigned to selves. James is not saying each self is a soul-substance; he is saying that, phenomenologically, thoughts are owned, appropriated, continuous with prior thoughts, and embedded in a personal history.

Second, thought is changing. Strictly speaking, no mental state recurs identically. Even if one sees the same object twice, the second seeing occurs in a different context, with different bodily conditions, memories, expectations, and surrounding fringes.

Third, thought is continuous. Consciousness does not consist of discrete beads. James’s stream metaphor is designed to overcome the associationist picture in which mental life is made of atomic sensations externally connected. Transitions, tendencies, feelings of relation, and “fringes” are themselves real parts of experience.

Fourth, thought is object-directed. James is not a solipsist. Mental life intends, refers to, means, and deals with things beyond itself. But he denies that this requires a separate act of transcendence performed by a special ego. A thought can know an object directly in context, by its relations, anticipations, and successful continuations.

Fifth, thought is selective. This may be the deepest feature. Consciousness highlights, ignores, holds, rejects, organizes, and values. James compares mind to a sculptor: the world of experience that matters to us is carved out of a vastly richer field. Reason, ethics, aesthetics, practical life, and perception itself are all selective.

The fifth principle is therefore: mental life is a continuous, changing, personal, object-directed, selective stream, not a sequence of atomic representations.

⸻

6. The fringe, relations, and the non-sensational parts of experience

James’s doctrine of the “fringe” is one of his most original contributions to philosophy of mind. Classical empiricism tended to treat experience as composed of clear, discrete sensations or ideas. James insists that much of mental life consists of vague tendencies, anticipations, feelings of relation, feelings of rightness or wrongness, directions of meaning, and contextual halos. The “fringe” is the felt background that gives definite contents their significance.

For example, when one follows a sentence, the meaning is not exhausted by the currently heard word. The whole intended sense is present as a felt direction or total idea, even as particular words pass. James’s account of thought therefore includes dynamic meaning before it is fully articulated.

This is important for contemporary philosophy of mind because it undermines the assumption that experience is made only of sensory qualia plus propositional judgments. James gives us a richer field model: focal contents are surrounded by penumbral relations, tendencies, and affective-motor orientations. The mind is not just a container of representations; it is an ongoing field of felt transitions.

This doctrine becomes metaphysically central in radical empiricism. James later argues that relations are not external intellectual additions to experience; relations are experienced. The conjunctions, transitions, continuities, and tendencies that connect experiences are as much part of reality as the terms they connect.

The sixth principle is: relations are felt, not merely inferred. The stream contains its own connective tissue.

⸻

7. Selfhood: material, social, spiritual, and the “pure ego”

James’s chapter on the self is a landmark because it refuses both simple ego-substance theory and simple bundle theory. He begins with the “empirical self,” meaning everything a person is tempted to call “me” or “mine”: body, clothes, family, property, reputation, projects, memories, social roles, and inner life.

He distinguishes the material self, the social self, the spiritual self, and the pure ego. The material self includes body, clothing, possessions, and extensions of bodily identity. The social self consists in recognition by others; a person has as many social selves as there are groups or persons whose recognition matters. The spiritual self is the inner subjective life, especially the felt stream of active consciousness.

The most delicate question is the “pure ego.” James treats it as the apparent center of appropriation, identity, and active unity. But he refuses to posit an inaccessible metaphysical ego if the facts can be explained by the way each present thought appropriates the preceding thought. Personal identity is not secured by an underlying soul-substance standing behind the stream; it is achieved within the stream, by continuity, memory, warmth, bodily feeling, and practical appropriation.

This does not make James a simple Humean. Hume tends to dissolve the self into a bundle of perceptions. James agrees that no separate ego-substance is found by introspection, but he thinks Hume misses the felt continuity, warmth, appropriation, bodily nucleus, and active orientation of the stream. James’s self is not a metaphysical substance, but neither is it a mere heap. It is a dynamically appropriative process.

He even locates much of the “central self” in bodily feelings, especially cephalic, throat, and motor adjustments accompanying attention and effort. The spiritual self may feel lofty, but its nucleus is often bodily and active.

The seventh principle is: the self is a layered, embodied, social, appropriative process within the stream, not an occult owner standing outside it.

⸻

8. “The Thought is the Thinker”: James’s anti-substantialist account of subjectivity

One of James’s boldest claims is that the present “Thought” is the only thinker required by the facts. The subject of experience is not an additional thing behind experience; it is the present passing thought insofar as it inherits, appropriates, owns, and continues the prior stream.

This claim must be read carefully. James is not saying there are thoughts without any subject at all. He is saying the subject is not an extra metaphysical entity over and above the stream’s own functional unity. A present thought can be subject by standing in relations of appropriation to past thoughts and anticipation toward future ones.

This has deep implications for philosophy of mind. It rejects the Cartesian picture of a permanent ego-substance. It also rejects purely impersonal empiricism. Subjectivity is real, but it is immanent to the stream. The “I” is not an object found alongside other objects; it is a function of ownership, attention, memory, bodily orientation, and practical continuity.

The eighth principle is: subjectivity is not a substance but a functional organization of experience.

⸻

9. Attention: selection as the essence of conscious life

Attention is the mechanism through which James’s selective view of mind becomes concrete. Attention is not merely a spotlight passively illuminating pre-given contents. It is active holding, returning, emphasizing, and inhibiting. Passive attention is captured by vivid or interesting objects; voluntary attention involves effort, especially the effort of repeatedly bringing a wandering mind back to its topic. James famously treats this capacity to return attention as the root of judgment, character, and will.

Attention also changes what is experienced. It fixes ideas, strengthens them, reorganizes the field, and makes some possibilities practically dominant. James links attention to bodily adjustment, but he refuses to reduce it entirely to muscular mechanisms. Attention includes inhibition and the active stabilization of a representation within the stream.

Attention is therefore not a minor psychological topic. It is the hinge between consciousness, agency, knowledge, selfhood, and morality. What we attend to becomes part of who we are. What we can hold before the mind determines what we can do.

The ninth principle is: attention is selective mental agency in its most elementary form.

⸻

10. Will: effort, consent, and the holding of an idea

James’s theory of will is one of the most philosophically important parts of Principles. He rejects the idea that will is primarily a mysterious force that directly pushes muscles. Voluntary movement often depends on guiding sensations, bodily feedback, and acquired motor pathways. In many cases, the idea of the end is enough to set the appropriate movement in motion; the body, through habit and sensorimotor organization, fills in the details.

The core of will is not muscular force but the effort of attention. Volition consists in holding one idea steadily before consciousness when rival ideas, impulses, fears, and habits compete. The decisive act is consent, fiat, adoption: “let it be.” James analyzes several kinds of decision, but the morally crucial case is action along the line of greatest resistance, where one ideal is sustained against contrary propensities.

This is James’s deepest compatibilist-indeterminist ambiguity. As a scientist, he admits that psychology can leave determinism methodologically in place. But as a moral philosopher, he insists that the lived reality of effort is central to personhood. The question of free will turns on whether the amount of effort of attention we put forth is itself wholly determined or can be genuinely indeterminate. James refuses to let scientific method prematurely decide that metaphysical question.

The tenth principle is: will is not a supernatural push inserted into nature; it is the effortful stabilization of an idea until it becomes effective in action.

⸻

11. Emotion: the James-Lange theory and embodied feeling

James’s theory of emotion is often summarized as “we do not cry because we are sad; we are sad because we cry.” That slogan is too simple, but it captures the reversal he intended. In his account, the perception of an emotionally significant object directly triggers bodily changes, and the felt awareness of those bodily changes is the emotion. Fear, anger, grief, and joy are not purely inner states later expressed by the body; they are bodily reverberations felt as conscious emotion.

James radicalizes this point by asking us to abstract away all bodily symptoms from an emotion. If one removes trembling, accelerated heartbeat, breathing changes, muscular tension, tears, visceral disturbance, and postural orientation, what remains of fear or anger? He concludes that disembodied human emotion is nearly a nonentity.

Again, this is not crude materialism. James allows that emotions can be aroused by memory and imagination, and he acknowledges complicated “spiritual” emotions. But even there he is cautious: he does not affirm a wholly bodiless emotion independent of bodily or cerebral conditions.

The James-Lange theory also has practical and ethical implications. Expression can intensify emotion; refusing expression can weaken passion. Bodily discipline can therefore affect moral and emotional life.

Contemporary Spanish-language work on James and the “affective turn” has emphasized precisely this anti-dualist aspect: James’s emotion theory resists treating cognition and emotion as two separate processes and instead suggests that lived experience is a unified process involving subjective feeling and bodily change.

The eleventh principle is: emotion is embodied feeling, not an inner mental event merely accompanied by bodily expression.

⸻

12. Habit, plasticity, and the temporal body

James’s doctrine of habit deserves independent attention because it gives his philosophy of mind a temporal and developmental depth. Habit shows that the body remembers. Repeated actions modify future action. A character is not merely a set of beliefs or choices; it is a bodily-neural history organized into tendencies.

Habit also explains how consciousness can be selective without being constantly burdened by detail. Once an action-chain is learned, consciousness may initiate or supervise it only at key points, while bodily feedback and acquired pathways handle the sequence.

This is not a reduction of agency to mechanism. It is an account of how agency becomes embodied. The will depends on habit, and habit depends on past will. A Jamesian philosophy of mind must therefore treat freedom and mechanism as interwoven. Habit mechanizes prior freedom; attention can interrupt habit and begin reorganization.

The twelfth principle is: mind is historically sedimented in habit, and habit is the bodily form of past attention and action.

⸻

13. The unconscious, dissociation, and the subliminal self

James’s position on the unconscious is complex and developmental. In Principles, he is sharply critical of “unconscious ideas” and “unconscious inferences” when they are invoked as mythological explanatory devices. He prefers to explain many such cases by unconscious brain processes rather than by fully mental ideas hidden below consciousness.

Yet James is also deeply interested in hypnosis, automatic writing, secondary personalities, dissociation, and pathological cases where consciousness appears to split. He discusses Janet, Binet, automatic writing, secondary consciousness, and systematized anesthesia. These cases complicate any simple equation of mind with one unified field.

In Varieties, this becomes the doctrine of the subliminal self. James uses subliminal activity to explain conversion and religious transformation without reducing them to fraud or pathology. The subliminal is a psychological region of hidden incubation, reorganization, and emergence. It may also, James speculates, be the point at which “higher” spiritual agencies could touch ordinary consciousness. Crucially, he presents this as a possibility, not as a demonstrated dogma.

So James has a layered view: he rejects lazy appeals to unconscious ideas, but he accepts that personal consciousness may be wider, more divided, and more porous than ordinary waking awareness suggests. This becomes indispensable for his treatment of religion.

The thirteenth principle is: consciousness is normally personal and continuous, but the personal field may be split, subliminally extended, or reorganized beneath ordinary awareness.

⸻

14. Religious experience and the refusal of “medical materialism”

In Varieties, James studies religion psychologically but refuses to explain away religious value by reducing it to pathology. He criticizes what he calls “medical materialism”: the view that, because a religious experience has a bodily or pathological condition, its meaning or truth-value is thereby discredited. For James, origin and value are different questions. A belief’s genesis does not automatically decide its worth.

This is a general principle in his philosophy of mind. All mental life has bodily conditions; if bodily conditioning discredited mental content, then science, philosophy, morality, and ordinary perception would be discredited too. James therefore separates psychological explanation from epistemic or spiritual evaluation.

Mystical states are especially important. James treats them as a serious chapter in human experience, marked by ineffability and noetic quality. He does not claim that mystical experiences prove a particular theology. But he does argue that they are psychologically real, often transformative, and not dismissible merely because they are unusual.

At the end of Varieties, James proposes a minimal religious hypothesis: human beings experience uneasiness, find solution through connection with higher powers, and may have a “higher part” of the self continuous with a “more” beyond ordinary consciousness. The “more” can be interpreted differently by different theologies, but psychologically it is connected with the subliminal extension of the self.

The fourteenth principle is: mental states must be evaluated by their experiential, practical, and transformative consequences, not merely by their bodily origins.

⸻

15. The critique of “mind-stuff” and the combination problem

James’s critique of mind-stuff theories is strikingly relevant to contemporary debates about panpsychism. The mind-stuff theory says that complex consciousness is composed out of smaller elementary mental units, perhaps attached to smaller physical units. James attacks this as inwardly unintelligible. How do many little feelings combine into one larger feeling? How can separate bits of consciousness produce a unified conscious field?

James’s answer is severe: the supposed composition is not explained. A motion becoming a feeling is unintelligible, but many little feelings becoming one big feeling is also unintelligible unless one simply posits the new total fact. Better to say that a total brain-process has one total feeling than to imagine that many mental dust-particles somehow fuse themselves into a subject.

This anticipates the modern “combination problem” for panpsychism: even if elementary units have proto-experience, how do they combine into unified subjects? James’s own later radical empiricism sometimes seems more hospitable to panpsychist or neutral-monist readings, but his critique of mind-stuff remains a major constraint. A Jamesian panpsychism cannot be a simple aggregation of mental atoms.

The fifteenth principle is: unified consciousness cannot be explained by merely summing smaller consciousnesses.

⸻

16. Radical empiricism: consciousness is not an entity

James’s late radical empiricism transforms the entire framework. In “Does Consciousness Exist?” he argues that “consciousness,” if taken as an entity distinct from the content of experience, is a fiction—an “echo” of the disappearing soul. He does not deny that knowing occurs. He denies that knowing requires a separate substance called consciousness. Consciousness is a function, not a stuff.

The basis of reality is “pure experience.” This does not mean private sensation. It means experience before the conceptual split into subject and object, mind and matter, inner and outer. A given portion of pure experience can function as “mental” in one relational context and as “physical” in another. The distinction between mental fire and physical fire, for example, is not a difference in two metaphysical stuffs but in relations, consequences, and contexts.

James’s radical empiricism has three key theses.

First, experience includes relations as well as terms. The transitions, continuities, conjunctions, and separations that connect experiences are themselves experienced and real. This repairs the atomism of older empiricism.

Second, knowing is not a mysterious leap outside experience. Knowing consists in experienced transitions that lead from one part of experience to another, with successful verification, fulfillment, or continuation. Knowledge is made within experience over time.

Third, reality is pluralistic and mosaic-like. There is no need to posit an Absolute Mind that pre-unifies everything. Relations may be many, partial, discontinuous, and practical.

This yields the sixteenth principle: mind and matter are not two ultimate substances; they are functional organizations within pure experience.

Portuguese scholarship on James’s radical empiricism has emphasized this exact point: the doctrine of consciousness as a function of knowing within experience is read as part of a pluralistic metaphysics that overcomes both mind-matter dualism and reductive naturalism.

⸻

17. Is James a neutral monist?

James is often classified as a neutral monist, and there is good reason for this. His radical empiricism denies that the world is ultimately divided into mental stuff and physical stuff. “Pure experience” is neutral with respect to that later distinction. The mental and physical are not substances but functional orders.

However, James’s neutral monism is not a clean, austere, logical doctrine in the style later associated with Bertrand Russell. It is pluralistic, temporal, practical, and experiential. Pure experience is not a homogeneous metaphysical paste. It is a field of concrete, qualitative, relational events. It is “neutral” only in the sense that its parts are not intrinsically stamped as mental or physical prior to their relations.

French-language work on James and radical empiricism has highlighted this relational reading: consciousness is not a being but a cognitive relation between portions of pure experience; an experience is described by its function, context, and network of transitions.

German and French comparative work on James and Bergson likewise stresses that James turns to prepsychological “pure experience” to escape the dualism built into psychology, and that this is better understood as a heterogeneous continuum of qualities and relations than as a single inert substance.

So the seventeenth principle is: James is a pluralistic neutral empiricist, not a materialist, not a Cartesian dualist, and not an absolute idealist.

⸻

18. The soul in Principles: unnecessary for psychology, not refuted by psychology

James’s treatment of the soul is often misunderstood. In Principles, he says the soul-theory can seem like the “line of least logical resistance”: if one wants to explain unity, identity, and appropriation, it is tempting to posit a soul affected by the brain. But James immediately brackets this as metaphysics. Psychology, as a natural science, should remain with empirical parallelism: mental facts and bodily conditions are correlated, but psychology should not pretend to solve the ultimate metaphysical question by positing an occult soul-substance.

Later in the self chapter, James argues that soul-substance is not needed to explain the facts of personal identity. The present thought appropriates past thoughts; the stream maintains continuity through memory, warmth, bodily feeling, and practical inheritance. If “soul” means only the unknown ground of possibility, it explains nothing psychologically. If it is invoked to mediate the relation between brain and mind, it becomes an unnecessary duplication.

This is not an atheistic or materialistic refutation of the soul. It is a methodological exclusion. James is saying: do not use the soul as a fake psychological mechanism. If one believes in a soul for religious or metaphysical reasons, psychology cannot simply disprove it. But psychology does not need it as an explanatory posit.

The eighteenth principle is: the soul is not a scientific explanation of mental unity, but it remains a possible metaphysical or religious over-belief.

⸻

19. Human Immortality: production, transmission, and incorporeal mind

Human Immortality is the key text for James’s explicit comments on incorporeal minds and survival. The lecture addresses the objection that modern physiology has destroyed belief in immortality by showing that thought depends on the brain. James’s answer is logical rather than dogmatic: dependence does not specify the kind of functional relation. A brain may produce consciousness, but it may also transmit, filter, release, or permit consciousness.

His analogy is that light may pass through a prism or aperture. If the aperture is destroyed, the light no longer appears in that form, but the source need not be destroyed. Similarly, if the brain is a transmissive organ, the death of the brain ends the embodied manifestation of a stream without necessarily annihilating the wider consciousness from which it came. James does not claim to prove this. He claims only that physiology does not logically rule it out.

The preface is especially important because James rejects the idea that his view must collapse into pantheistic absorption. He explicitly says there may be many minds “behind the scenes,” not merely one, and that finite consciousness may be an extract from a larger and truer personality while still allowing individualized survival.

He also considers the objection that immortality would require room for innumerable beings. His reply is democratic and expansive: if any creature can live forever, why not all? Possible consciousness is not a conserved physical quantity that must fit into a container.

The notes to Human Immortality are revealing. James says he is using an ordinary dualistic standpoint for the purpose of the lecture, and he distinguishes production, combination, and separation/transmission views. He brackets mind-stuff because he had criticized it in Principles.

So James’s view on incorporeal mind is permissive but not demonstrative. He allows that consciousness might be more extensive than the brain, that the brain might restrict or transmit it, and that survival after bodily death is logically compatible with physiological dependence. But this is not his everyday empirical psychology; it is a metaphysical option defended against premature materialist closure.

The nineteenth principle is: brain-dependence does not logically entail brain-production; incorporeal or post-mortem mind remains possible, though not scientifically established by psychology.

⸻

20. James, Fechner, and the “mother sea” of consciousness

James sometimes entertains wider consciousness hypotheses, influenced by figures such as Gustav Fechner and Frederic Myers. In Human Immortality, he discusses threshold models, where our ordinary consciousness is a limited emergence from a wider psychical sea. The metaphor of a “mother sea” suggests that finite minds might be local expressions, openings, or transmissions of a more extensive field.

In Varieties, this becomes the idea that the conscious person is continuous with a “more” beyond ordinary awareness. The “more” first appears psychologically as the subliminal extension of the self; beyond that, theological and metaphysical interpretations begin.

This is the point at which James comes closest to a spiritual metaphysics of mind. But he remains pluralist. He does not simply identify the “more” with a single Absolute Mind, as some idealists would. Nor does he reduce it to brain processes. The “more” is an experiential and practical postulate, approached through conversion, mystical states, subliminal phenomena, and religious transformation.

The twentieth principle is: James allows a porous boundary between finite consciousness and wider consciousness, but he interprets this pluralistically and pragmatically rather than as a finished theological system.

⸻

21. Multilingual reception: what non-Anglophone sources foreground

A broader reception history helps bring out aspects of James that are sometimes underemphasized in Anglophone philosophy of mind.

French reception has often emphasized James’s proximity to phenomenology and Bergson. A French study notes that France knew James through figures such as Renouvier and Bergson, but that the lack of a French translation of Principles meant French philosophers often missed the proto-phenomenological importance of that work, even though it influenced Husserl and Wittgenstein.

French discussions of radical empiricism also stress that consciousness is not a being but a relation internal to experience. This is important because it keeps James from being misread as a psychologist who merely describes inner states. His mature view is ontological: the subject-object split itself is derivative.

Spanish-language work has been especially useful on James’s theory of emotion. Recent Spanish scholarship connects James to the affective turn in psychology, arguing that his view challenges the separation of cognition and emotion by treating experience as one embodied process. Spanish presentations of radical empiricism likewise stress the break with dualism: matter/spirit and subject/object are not primordial divisions but later functional differentiations within pure experience.

There is also a sharply critical Spanish-language Marxist reception, inherited from Soviet philosophical dictionaries, which labels James an idealist and fideist, emphasizing his “pure experience,” voluntarism, and religious openness. This hostile reading is not neutral, but it usefully shows how James’s anti-reductionism and religious permissiveness were read by materialist critics as concessions to idealism.

Portuguese-language scholarship on radical empiricism foregrounds consciousness as a function of knowledge within experience and treats James’s metaphysics as an attempt to overcome both dualism and reductionism. This reception is particularly helpful because it reads James’s psychology and metaphysics together rather than separating the “scientific psychologist” from the “religious pragmatist.”

German-language material, including recent scholarship, tends to foreground the dynamism of James’s mind-body theory: consciousness is temporally embedded, environmentally engaged, and inseparable from questions of action, freedom, and teleology. Some German discussion of James and the soul also emphasizes that he replaces the traditional substantial soul with consciousness, subliminal life, neutral monism, or even Fechnerian all-soul possibilities, while still allowing soul-language pragmatically when it affects life and action.

Finally, Japanese reception through Nishida Kitarō is significant. French-language scholarship on Nishida notes that Nishida borrowed the notion of “pure experience” from William James, especially “A World of Pure Experience,” and used it as a foundation for overcoming solipsism and articulating a unified account of reality, knowledge, and will. This does not mean Nishida’s system is James’s system, but it shows how James’s philosophy of mind could become the seed of a non-dual ontology outside the Anglophone tradition.

⸻

22. Systematic reconstruction: the Jamesian philosophy of mind

We can now extract a comprehensive philosophy of mind from James. The following theses form a coherent Jamesian system.

First, the ontological thesis: the mental and the physical are not two ultimate substances. At the empirical-psychological level, mental life is correlated with bodily and neural processes. At the radical-empiricist level, both mind and matter are functional organizations of pure experience. Consciousness is not a stuff but a role, function, or relation within experience.

Second, the phenomenological thesis: mental life is a stream. It is personal, changing, continuous, object-directed, and selective. Its fringes, transitions, and felt relations are real constituents of experience, not secondary interpretations imposed by intellect.

Third, the biological thesis: mind is an organ of adaptation, selection, and conduct. Consciousness arises where flexible response, uncertain conditions, inhibition, and choice matter. It is not an inert epiphenomenon.

Fourth, the embodiment thesis: perception, emotion, attention, action, habit, and selfhood are bodily. The body is not an external vehicle for an inner mind; it is woven into the structure of mental life. Emotions are felt bodily reverberations; habits are bodily histories; the self’s nucleus is often felt in bodily activity.

Fifth, the agency thesis: will is the effort of attention. Freedom, insofar as it is experientially accessible, appears in the capacity to hold one idea, ideal, or possibility against competing tendencies until it becomes effective.

Sixth, the selfhood thesis: the self is not a simple substance but a layered process of appropriation. It includes material, social, and spiritual dimensions; its unity is produced by memory, continuity, bodily warmth, recognition, and present thought’s inheritance of past thought.

Seventh, the epistemological thesis: knowing is not a mysterious relation between an inner idea and an outer object. Knowing occurs within experience through successful transitions, fulfillments, verifications, and practical continuations.

Eighth, the anti-reductionist thesis: bodily conditions do not settle metaphysical meaning. A mental state’s neural condition does not by itself discredit its truth, value, or spiritual import. This applies to religion, morality, science, and philosophy alike.

Ninth, the soul thesis: psychology does not need the soul as an explanatory entity, but metaphysics may still entertain soul, transmission, subliminal, or wider-consciousness hypotheses. These must be treated as over-beliefs or speculative possibilities, not as replacements for empirical work.

Tenth, the pluralism thesis: reality is not guaranteed to form one complete rational system from the start. Minds, relations, selves, and worlds may be many, partially connected, and unfinished. James’s philosophy of mind is therefore inseparable from his pluralism.

A compact label for the resulting view would be: pluralistic embodied process functionalism grounded in radical empiricism. It is functionalist because mind is understood by what it does in life. It is embodied because bodily processes are internal to emotion, habit, action, and selfhood. It is processual because the basic form of mind is stream, transition, and becoming. It is pluralistic because it rejects both atomistic empiricism and monistic absolutism. It is radically empirical because it accepts every experienced relation, fringe, transition, and value as part of what philosophy must account for.

⸻

23. Major tensions in James’s view

James’s philosophy of mind is powerful partly because it preserves tensions rather than prematurely resolving them.

The first tension is between psychology and metaphysics. In Principles, James often brackets metaphysical questions and insists on empirical psychology. In radical empiricism, he openly offers a metaphysical reconstruction of experience. This is not exactly a contradiction; it is a shift in level. Psychology studies mental life under bodily and temporal conditions. Radical empiricism asks what “mental” and “physical” mean in the first place.

The second tension is between brain-dependence and spiritual openness. James accepts that embodied mental life depends on brain function. Yet he argues that this does not prove production rather than transmission. The tension is deliberate: James wants the empirical facts without materialist overreach.

The third tension is between the rejection of unconscious ideas and the later use of the subliminal self. James rejects “unconscious ideas” when they are explanatory myths, but he accepts dissociation, secondary consciousness, and subliminal processes when they are supported by pathological, hypnotic, or religious evidence.

The fourth tension is between the bodily theory of emotion and the possibility of higher spiritual states. James’s solution is to say that spiritual significance does not require disembodied origin. A state may be bodily conditioned and still spiritually meaningful.

The fifth tension is between “the Thought is the Thinker” and the religious possibility of wider consciousness. At the psychological level, the present thought suffices as thinker. At the metaphysical-religious level, the finite stream may be continuous with a wider field. The two claims conflict only if one assumes that empirical psychology must exhaust metaphysics.

These tensions are not defects to be removed. They are the architecture of James’s position.

⸻

24. Relation to contemporary philosophy of mind

James anticipates several contemporary positions without fitting neatly into any one of them.

He anticipates functionalism by defining mind through adaptive role, selection, means, ends, and conduct. But he avoids thin computational functionalism by insisting on felt life, bodily emotion, fringe, effort, and value.

He anticipates enactivism and embodied cognition by making perception, emotion, will, and selfhood bodily and action-oriented. His mind is not an internal representational machine sealed off from the world.

He anticipates phenomenology by treating the stream, intentionality, temporality, fringe, and lived continuity as primary. French reception has rightly emphasized that the untranslated Principles contains a proto-phenomenological richness that influenced later European thought.

He anticipates neutral monism by rejecting mind and matter as ultimate substances. But his neutral monism is experiential and pluralistic, not merely logical.

He anticipates panpsychist debates by exposing the combination problem in mind-stuff theories. Yet he does not settle into standard panpsychism.

He anticipates affect theory by treating emotion as embodied, expressive, and constitutive of experience rather than as a secondary coloring of cognition.

He anticipates pragmatist epistemology by treating knowing as a process of transition, verification, and practical fulfillment within experience.

A contemporary Jamesian philosophy of mind would therefore resist the dominant alternatives: it would reject reductive physicalism, Cartesian dualism, sense-datum empiricism, computational abstractionism, and absolute idealism. It would instead begin with lived, embodied, selective, relational experience.

⸻

25. Final synthesis: James on mind, soul, and experience

James’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one governing idea: mind is not a thing behind experience; it is the living organization of experience itself.

In empirical psychology, that organization appears as the stream of thought: personal, continuous, changing, selective, object-directed, embodied, affective, attentive, and volitional.

In physiological psychology, it appears as brain-conditioned, habit-formed, sensorimotor, and environmentally adaptive.

In moral psychology, it appears as effort: the capacity to sustain an ideal possibility against competing impulses.

In the theory of emotion, it appears as bodily feeling: the organism reverberating in response to the world.

In the theory of self, it appears as appropriation: the present thought inheriting and owning the past.

In radical empiricism, it appears as pure experience organized by relations: subject and object, mind and matter, knower and known emerge within the same experiential field.

In religious psychology, it appears as openness: the finite conscious field may be continuous with subliminal depths and perhaps with wider spiritual realities.

The soul, then, is neither simply affirmed nor simply denied. As a psychological explanation, it is unnecessary. As a metaphysical hypothesis, it is not refuted by empirical psychology. As a religious over-belief, it may orient conduct, hope, and interpretation. As a transmissive hypothesis, it remains logically compatible with brain-dependence. But James’s deepest move is to replace the old question, “What substance is the mind made of?” with a better one: “How does experience organize itself into knowing, acting, feeling, selfhood, and world?”

That is the philosophy of mind James leaves us: not a doctrine of an immaterial ego inhabiting a machine, and not a doctrine of neural machinery producing an illusion of inner life, but a pluralistic account of experience becoming personal, bodily, meaningful, active, and possibly more extensive than ordinary consciousness knows.

PreviousMaurice Merleau-Ponty NextGilbert Ryle

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
